Documentary / business records. Records compiled in the ordinary course of a business are admissible as evidence of the facts in them under ss.5–6 Criminal Evidence Act 1992, on proof of the statutory conditions and usually by certificate (see §11). s.t: cases) Citation: Application No. 36636/14, decision 30 May 2017 (also Austin v UK 2012 and the principal kettling line of authority). Facts: Applicants were contained ('kettled') by police during demonstrations in London. Containment lasted several hours. Issue: whether containment engaged Article 5 (liberty) and if so whether proportionate. Holding: Police containment for crowd-control purposes may, depending on circumstances, fall outside the scope of Article 5 (Austin) or engage it (other cases). Where Article 5 engaged, containment must be necessary, proportionate, and time-limited. Reasoning: The Court distinguished containment driven by genuine public-order necessity from arbitrary detention. The duration, conditions, and necessity of containment are critical. Significance: Kettling / containment is operationally sensitive. Its use must be justified, proportionate, and bounded in time. Operational Application: Containment as a pub

s.t: It is now further structured by s.18 NFOAP 1997 and s.2 Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011. Operational Application: In any case where self-defence is in issue: (a) investigate the IP’s conduct toward the accused — was there a perceived threat; (b) document the accused’s account of their belief at the time; (c) measure the force used objectively against the circumstances as the accused subjectively believed them — weapon used, location of blows, repetition, duration; (d) consider the excessive self-defence partial defence — a case may be a murder if neither limb met, but a manslaughter if the first limb met and the second not. s.t: Visual identification is substantially stronger where a person recognises an individual who is familiar to them from past meetings, associations and so on. See note at People (DPP) v Behan at 6.17 1st February 1992 CCA. 6.6. Improper Methods of Visual Identification The following methods of visual identification should not be adopted: • Direct confrontation: that is showing a suspect alone to a witness or otherwise pointing out the suspect to a witness and asking the witness if they can identify them. This method was used in

If, for example, the suspect is of singular appearance it may not be possible to get together sufficient people to make up a fair parade. The holding of an identification parade would probably be a redundant exercise if the witness knew the suspect previously. In The People (DPP) v Behan (CCA, Ex tempore, February 1, 1993) the Court recognised that a formal identification parade is not required where the witness may already know the accused. The Courts are not concerned with a situation where a suspect will not agree to go on an identification parade or attempts to frustrate it once it is assembled. If that is the course a suspect takes, then that is the particular option that they have exercised and they may have to live with the consequences. However, the situation in the case of D.P.P. v O’Reilly is that the accused man was given no such option at all. 6.14. Constitutional Right to Fair Procedures In the case of

The suspect had refused to take part in an identification parade and therefore he could not expect the usual procedures in Court to be altered and for him to thereby gain some benefit. In the case of The People (DPP) v Behan (1 February 1992 CCA) the injured party recognised the suspect at the time of the incident. Some days later she was brought to the Children’s Court in Smithfield and in the precincts of the Court she picked out the suspect from a number of people who simply happened to be there at the time. It was argued that the evidence of the informal identification should not have been admitted at all. The Court stated that this was a recognition case rather than an identification case. The Court held that the trial judge had given a very strong warning to the jury in accordance with the Casey criteria. The visual identification evidence was admitted. The Court did, however, emphasise that if the parties had not known each other

d: In The People (DPP) v Behan (CCA, Ex tempore, February 1, 1993) the Court recognised that a formal identification parade is not required where the witness may already know the accused.

s.t: Dishonestly “Dishonestly” means without a claim of right made in good faith (Section 2(1)). In the People [AG] v Grey, 1944 I.R. 326 and R v Bernard [1938] 2 KB 264 the claim of right made in good faith was held to be one that need not be recognised in law if the accused honestly believed that the claim was justified. In Grey the claim of the accused was based on an unenforceable contract but this was held to negate the required element of fraud. Similarly in the People [DPP] v O’Loughlin [1979] I.R. 85 the belief by the accused that he was entitled to remove goods in order to enforce payment of a debt which was owed to him was held to be good. Where it appears to the investigating member that such a defence will arise, a preliminary file should be submitted to the Law Officers for directions. The file should include full details, supported by relevant statements outlining where the defence put forward by the suspect will arise. Cases o

s.t: Applied in DPP v Bowe regarding entry to a 'part of a building'. R v Brown [1985] Crim LR 212 (CA) Effective entry sufficient for burglary — partial bodily entry into building. Threshold for 'enters as a trespasser' is low.

Treating property as the accused's own to dispose of regardless of the owner's rights satisfies the test (s.4(5)). Operational application: When taking a statement from a suspect, capture every fact that bears on disposal — where they left the property, what condition, whether they tried to return it. In the file, list every objective circumstance that supports the inference. DPP v Bowe [2017] IECA 215 Facts: Bowe entered a shop and crossed into the staff-only area behind the counter without consent. He removed money from a till. The defence argued the shop was a public place and consent to enter was general. Held: The Court of Appeal held that consent to enter a public premises does not extend to areas from which the public is excluded. Crossing into a 'part of a building' (the staff-only area) without consent constituted entry as a trespasser, sufficient to ground a burglary count alongside theft. Note: where entry crosses into a staff-only area, also consider burglary contrary to s.12 (DPP v Bowe; R v Walkington). Legal Framework All elements of theft apply (1.0). Specific operational considerations: • Asportation — the slightest movement suffices (Thompson). The offence is complete inside the shop. • Concealment is strong evidence of dishonest intent. Document it precisely. • 'Pre-meditation' or 'shopping with intent' (booster bags, foil-lined coats) is an aggravating factor for sentence and may ground a 'going equipped' charge under s.15. • Where security detains the suspect at the door, the detention is a citizen's arrest under s.4 Criminal Law Act 1997 (anyone may arrest a person committing

s.t: Where a formal ID procedure (parade or VIPER) could have been conducted and was not, dock ID should be approached with extreme caution. Why It Matters: Always offer a formal ID procedure where the witness has not previously identified the suspect. Document the offer and any refusal. s.t: Established the Casey warning: where a conviction depends wholly or substantially on visual identification, the jury must be warned of the danger of acting on it, and of the possibility that an honest and convincing witness may nonetheless be mistaken. People (DPP) v Boyce [2008] IESC 62 (adopting R v Turnbull [1977] QB 224) Holding / principle. The court must direct the jury to examine the circumstances of the identification using the Turnbull factors, captured operationally by ADVOKATE: Amount of time under observation, Distance, Visibility, Obstruction, Known to the witness, Any reason to remember, Time lapse since the event, Errors or discrepancies between accounts. Operational point. Take the ADVOKATE detail in the witness statement at the time, in the witness’s own words — not reconstructed later. Operational application: Store security and your statement should record the precise moment of appropriation — where the goods were taken from, where the suspect moved them to, whether they were concealed. The suspect's departure from the premises is corroborative but not necessary. DPP v Brennan [1998] 4 IR 67 Facts: Brennan was found in possession of property that had been stolen recently. He could not give a reasonable account of how he came to possess it. Held: The Court applied the doctrine of recent possession: unexplained possession of recently stolen property can ground an inference that the possessor either stole it or received it knowing it stolen. The inference is rebuttable but the burden of providing a credible explanation is practical. Ratio: Recent possession + absence of credible explanation = available inference of theft or handling. s.t: B.3.3 Cross-examination — the rules behind Part A Cross-examination may go to the issues and to credit. Counsel must put their case: matters they intend to contradict must be put to the witness so the witness can answer them (the rule in Browne v Dunn). Answers on purely collateral matters (going only to credit) are generally final and cannot be contradicted, subject to exceptions (previous convictions, bias, reputation for untruthfulness, physical/mental condition affecting reliability).

s.t: C.2 The People (DPP) v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 Citation: [2002] 2 IR 268 (Court of Criminal Appeal). Facts: Buck was charged with murder. The prosecution relied on inferences from his silence in interview under s.18 CJA 1984. The trial judge's directions and the prerequisites of the inference were challenged. Holding: The statutory prerequisites for adverse inferences from silence must be strictly observed. The accused must be cautioned in the prescribed terms, given a reasonable opportunity to consult a solicitor, and the question put must fall within the statutory scope. Deviation invalidates the inference. Reasoning: The Court emphasised that inferences from silence are an inroad on the right to silence and must be tightly bounded by statutory compliance. The warning is not a formality - it is the procedural protection that justifies the inferential consequence. Significance: Strict compliance regime for s.18, s.19, s.19A CJA 1984 infer

Buck-strict compliance: prescribed warning; pre-interview consultation; question within statutory scope; recorded response. A.4

s.t: A.2 The People (DPP) v Cagney & McGrath [2008] 2 IR 111 Citation: [2008] 2 IR 111; [2007] IESC 46 (Supreme Court). Facts: Two accused convicted of manslaughter following a single-punch death outside a takeaway. Issue on appeal: trial judge's direction on recklessness in unlawful-act manslaughter. Holding: The Supreme Court reaffirmed Murray. Recklessness is subjective. The accused must have been aware of the risk of serious harm and proceeded to take it. The reasonable-person standard is the objective check against the accused's account, not the test of liability. Reasoning: Geoghegan J (with Fennelly and Kearns JJ concurring) emphasised that the subjective standard is the settled Irish position and rejected any drift toward objective recklessness following English Caldwell developments and subsequent retraction. Significance: Reaffirmation at the highest level of the Murray subjective standard. The standard is now beyond doubt in Irish

s.t: Part D — Identification (Casey Warning) The People (AG) v Casey (No 2) [1963] IR 33 — Visual Identification Warning Citation: [1963] IR 33 (Court of Criminal Appeal). Facts: The accused was convicted on the basis of visual identification evidence. The CCA considered the dangers of identification evidence and the appropriate warning to be given to a jury where the prosecution case rested wholly or substantially on visual identification. Holding: Where the case depends wholly or substantially on visual identification, the trial judge must give the jury a specific warning about the dangers of relying on visual identification: that honest and convinced witnesses can be mistaken; that previous reliability of the witness in other respects does not eliminate this risk; that the jury must consider the circumstances of the identification (lighting, distance, duration of observation, prior familiarity). Reasoning: The court recognised the well-do

C.3 The People (AG) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33 Citation: [1963] IR 33 (Supreme Court). Facts: Casey was convicted on visual identification evidence. The conviction was challenged on the basis that the jury had not been adequately warned about the dangers of visual identification. Holding: Where the prosecution case depends substantially on visual identification, the trial judge must give a specific warning to the jury about the dangers of identification evidence. The warning must alert the jury that even honest witnesses can be mistaken and that miscarriages of justice have occurred through such mistakes. Reasoning: The Court (Kingsmill Moore J) drew on common-law experience of identification mistakes leading to wrongful conviction. The warning is a structural protection at trial against the inherent risk. Significance: The Casey warning is mandatory where visual identification is substantial. Failure to give it is grounds for quashing

Facts: Applicant was injured by police during the dispersal of a school occupation at the 2001 Genoa G8 summit. Severe injuries inflicted by baton strikes. Holding: Violation of Article 3 (prohibition of torture, inhuman or degrading treatment). The use of force was excessive, unnecessary, and constituted ill-treatment. Italy also breached the procedural obligation under Article 3 to investigate effectively. Reasoning: The Court found the conduct rose to the threshold of torture given the severity and the lack of necessity. The investigative response was inadequate. Article 3 imposes both substantive limits on force and procedural duties of investigation. Significance: Force at demonstrations engaging Article 3 where excessive. s.t: Christie v FACTS: Leachinsky arrested under a local Leachinsky [1947] Act without being told the true reason for arrest. The House of Lords held the arrest AC 573 (HL) unlawful and amounted to false imprisonment. PRINCIPLE: Viscount Simon set out that a person arrested must be informed of the true ground of arrest, in language they can understand, save where: (a) they already know (caught red-handed), (b) it is impossible to communicate (resisting, fleeing) — must be told as soon as practicable, (c) the person makes communication impossible by their own conduct. RATIO: Liberty being deprived, the citizen has a right to know why. Without the reason, the arrest is unlawful. The case is consistently applied in Irish law. TEST: Was the substance of the offence communicated in terms the suspect could understand at or as soon as practicable after the arrest?

s.t: Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power Power of arrest: • s.4 Criminal Law Act 1997 — arrestable offence (theft is punishable by 5 years or more on indictment; s.4 applies). • Without warrant where reasonable suspicion is held by the arresting member personally (DPP v Walsh). • On arrest, state the offence in plain terms (Christie v Leachinsky / DPP v Pringle), administer caution, identify yourself, and convey to a Garda station. Where the suspect is detained at scene by store security or by a bystander, the detention is lawful only if reasonable; immediate Garda attendance is essential. Document the time of detention and the time of arrest separately. A prolonged civilian detention can ground a defence challenge under Article 5 ECHR. Interview Plan PEACE-aligned. s.t: Common law does not favour formal identification for the first time in Court (dock identification), since by this stage in the proceedings the witness may feel under pressure to make a positive identification. In The People (DPP) v Cooney [1998] 1 ILRM 321 (SC) the Supreme Court held that Irish law has for a long time recognised that so called ‘dock identifications’ are undesirable and unsatisfactory, for the reason that the identification of the accused for the first time when he or she is sitting in the place normally reserved for the accused and usually flanked by prison officers is of limited probative value. For that reason, in cases where the identification of the accused is likely to be at issue, the appropriate procedure is to hold an identification parade or, if that is impracticable, to afford the identification witness some other opportunity of identifying the accused. 1.5 Recurring Pitfalls — Snapshots from Real Cases These short entries capture recurring mid-level mistakes that have surfaced in case law. Each can sink a case — and each is preventable with discipline. DPP v Cullen — Wrong section cited (illustrative composite) FACTS: Arresting member cited s.30 OASA in the heat of the moment for an offence that was not scheduled. The defective characterisation was challenged. PRINCIPLE: Citing the wrong power at arrest can be fatal where the cited power does not in fact authorise the arrest. Where a different power did authorise it, courts may sometimes excuse — but the safest course is to cite correctly first time. PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Know your powers. Don't say 'section 30' if you mean 'section 4'. Standard arrestable offence — s.4 CJA 1984. Public order — s.30 CJ(PO)A 1994. Scheduled OASA — s.30 OASA 1939. Drugs — typically s.4 CJA 1984 for underlying MDA offence. Drunk driving — s.49(8) RTA 19

DPP v Cullen FACTS: Suspect questioned without caution, made admissions; later cautioned [2014] IECCA 4 and re-questioned. PRINCIPLE: Admissions before caution inadmissible. Later admissions, even with caution, tainted unless the prosecution showed the breach did not influence the later statement. RATIO: A missed caution does not just exclude the immediate answer; it can taint the entire interview. The cure is to caution, advise the suspect that anything previously said cannot be used, and start over.

d: DPP v Cullen — Wrong section cited (illustrative composite) FACTS: Arresting member cited s.30 OASA in the heat of the moment for an offence that was not scheduled.

s.t: 2. The Proper Approach in Irish Law The People (DPP) v Cumberton [2020] IECA 136 Holding. The leading modern restatement. The case (the fatal shooting of Michael Barr at the Sunset House, Summerhill Parade, Dublin 1) was wholly circumstantial. The Court of Appeal reiterated and clarified how circumstantial evidence is to be approached: the tribunal must assess the evidence as a whole, not strand by strand in isolation, and convict only if satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that guilt is the only reasonable inference from the proven facts taken together. Individual strands need not each be proved beyond reasonable doubt; it is the ultimate inference of guilt that must reach that standard — save that a fact which is an indispensable link in the chain of reasoning must itself be proved beyond reasonable doubt. Operational point. Build and present the case as a whole. Map how the strands converge; do not let the file read as a list of unconn

s.t: The defence in multi-suspect cases routinely carves out individual clients on foresight grounds; the prosecution must build the individualised foresight picture. DPP v Cumberton (Court of Criminal Appeal) — Withdrawal from Joint Enterprise Facts and Holding: The court considered the doctrine of withdrawal from a joint enterprise — the proposition that a secondary party who effectively withdraws from the common design before the principal’s act of harm can avoid liability. Significance: Withdrawal must be effective, timely, and communicated. Mere mental dissociation is insufficient. The withdrawing party must take steps to neutralise their previous contribution where possible. Operational Application: Where any accused claims withdrawal, investigate: what they said / did to communicate withdrawal; whether they attempted to prevent the offence; whether their previous contributions were neutralised. s.t: It is excluded if obtained by oppression, or by a threat or inducement held out by a person in authority, or in circumstances that sap the free exercise of the suspect’s will. Voluntariness is decided by the judge on the voir dire and the prosecution bears the onus of proving it. See People (AG) v Cummins [1972] IR 312 and People (DPP) v Shaw [1982] IR 1. Separate from voluntariness, the court retains a discretion to exclude a voluntary statement where, on the whole circumstances, admitting it would be unfair to the accused. • Oppression — conduct that saps the will: prolonged or hostile questioning, deprivation of rest, food or medical attention, intimidation. • Inducement — a threat or promise by a person in authority (“it’ll go easier if you talk”, “you can go home once you sign”). Even a well-intentioned hint can vitiate voluntariness. • Capacity — intoxication, withdrawal, youth, or mental disorder bear on whether the statement was

Confessions obtained by false pretence, trick or fraud A confession obtained by means of a false pretence or trick is not on that basis alone inadmissible in evidence but it will be if the effect of the false pretence or trick has been to obtain an involuntary confession. Similarly, if there is any element of compulsion, this will also lead to the exclusion of the evidence – People (AG) v Cummins (1972) I.R. 312. Crime Investigation Techniques Manual 9

d: See People (AG) v Cummins [1972] IR 312 and People (DPP) v Shaw [1982] IR 1.

s.t: Key Authority DPP v Mahalingam [1995] 2 IR 117 — Unlawful Act Manslaughter Holding: Unlawful act must be objectively dangerous — a sober and reasonable person would recognise the risk of some harm. The accused need not have appreciated the risk. Why It Matters: Highly relevant to one-punch cases. Objective dangerousness, not subjective appreciation. DPP v Curran [2011] IECCA — Gross Negligence Manslaughter Holding: Requires (a) duty of care, (b) breach amounting to gross negligence, (c) breach creating a serious and obvious risk of death, (d) breach causing death. Working reference - not legal advice - verify before relying. | Page 120

Critical in one-punch killings, drunken-fight fatalities, and any case where an accused’s assault unexpectedly results in death. Operational Application: Investigation in one-punch / fight fatalities: (a) establish the unlawful act with full assault-investigation rigour; (b) establish the objective dangerousness (a punch to the head is widely understood as dangerous; the prosecution case is usually self-evident on this); (c) establish causation — pathologist evidence linking the blow to the death; (d) do not get sidetracked by the accused’s subjective non-appreciation; Mahalingam is objective on this limb. DPP v Curran [2011] IECCA — Gross Negligence Manslaughter Citation: [2011] IECCA (Court of Criminal Appeal). Facts: The accused was a professional whose conduct, alleged to amount to gross negligence, contributed to the death of a person to whom a duty of care was owed. Appellate Authority Where Defence Won • DPP v Cunningham [2012] IECCA 64 — evidence excluded where warrant defects amounted to constitutional breach (pre-JC, but instructive). • DPP v Damache [2012] IESC 11 — s.29 OASA warrant struck down; evidence from that route inadmissible. •

The investigative member’s state of mind at the time of the breach is now central. Why It Matters: Document your reasoning at the time of every warrant application, every search, every seizure. If a later defect is discovered, you want your notebook to evidence good faith — not afterthought rationalisation. DPP v Damache [2012] IESC 11 — Invalidity of s.29 OASA Warrants Facts: Search warrant under s.29 OASA 1939 issued by a Superintendent who was part of the investigation. Constitutional challenge. Holding: The Supreme Court held that a warrant issued by a person who is part of the investigating team violates the independent and impartial issuing-authority requirement implicit in Article 40.5 of the Constitution. S.29 was struck down. Significance: Warrants must be issued by an authority independent of the investigation — typically a District Judge or, for limited s.10 CJA 2006 applications, a Peace Commissioner. Why It Matters: Always

s.t: R v Dawson and James (1976) 64 Cr App R 170 Robbery — use of force on the person can be minor (jostling sufficient). Threshold for 'force' in s.14 is not high.

If contact is purely incidental (bag-snap that catches the IP’s arm), it may be theft from person. If the suspect pulls the IP forward, knocks them off-balance, or pushes them, it is robbery. R v Dawson [1976] (English authority — persuasive) Holding: Even minor force used to overcome resistance is sufficient. The use of force need not cause injury. Why It Matters: Cited approvingly in Irish courts. The jostling or shouldering of an IP to facilitate a pocketdip can suffice.

Charge begging only where harassment, intimidation or obstruction is present and evidenced. Full treatment: p.858 C.C. v Ireland Ratio. Absolute-liability “statutory rape” (no honest-mistake-as-to-age defence) was unconstitutional; produced the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006. Apply it. The reason the modern child-offence regime carries an age-belief defence. Full treatment: p.877 S.H. v DPP Ratio. Delay in sexual cases: the test is whether there is a real risk of an unfair trial, not the reasons for the complainant’s delay. Apply it. Historic cases are prosecutable — locate surviving material and address specific prejudice. Full treatment: p.877 The People (DPP) v C O'R Ratio. Belief in consent (rape): an honest, even unreasonable, mist

2. The Real Irish Authorities Dillon v DPP [2007] IEHC (High Court, de Valera J) Holding / principle. Section 3 of the Vagrancy (Ireland) Act 1847 — the old offence of begging in a public place — was struck down as unconstitutional. An absolute ban on begging in any public place in all circumstances breached the right to freedom of expression (Article 40.6.1°), and the offence was so vague and arbitrary as to offend Articles 34.1, 40.1, 40.3 and 40.4.1°. The court accepted that begging may be regulated as to manner and circumstance, but not banned outright. Operational point. The vagrancy-era begging power is gone. Begging is now an offence only under the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 2011, and only where accompanied by aggravating conduct — harassment, intimidation, threats, assault, or obstruction of a person or vehicle. Charge it only where that aggravating conduct is present and evidenced; mere begging is not an offence. Worki

s.t: Your job is not to win the exchange; it is to give accurate evidence and let it stand. In re Haughey [1971] IR 217 — fair procedures include the right to cross-examine those who give evidence against a person. Donnelly v Ireland [1998] 1 IR 321 — the right to cross-examine is a component of the right to a fair trial, though it is not unqualified (special-measures evidence, live link, etc.). The objectives the advocate is pursuing • To weaken the weight of your evidence (perception, memory, certainty). • To attack your credibility or suggest bias or motive. • To expose procedural or investigative gaps (caution, search, continuity, disclosure). • To undermine identification. • To obtain admissions through compound, loaded or rapid-fire questions. THE GOLDEN RULE. Consistency between your notebook, your statement and your oral evidence is your armour. Cross-examination has very little to work with when all three align. Everything in Part A

why: No constitutional right to have a solicitor PRESENT during interview (though it is now standard practice by regulation/DPP direction). Distinguishes Gormley.
d: Interviews and Admissions DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 — Right to Lawyer During Questioning Facts: Suspect questioned without solicitor; admissions made.

s.t: A suspect who has requested legal advice must not be questioned until that advice has been obtained; evidence (including forensic samples in White) taken in breach is liable to exclusion. The right to legal advice attaches before and around questioning, not merely on paper. People (DPP) v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 Holding / principle. Considered whether there is a right to have a solicitor physically present throughout interview. The position is nuanced; current Garda practice permits solicitor presence on request. Record precisely what access and presence were offered and provided. Verify the current operational instruction before interview. Corroboration warning for confessions. Where a conviction would rest wholly or substantially on a confession, s.10 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1993 requires the trial judge to consider warning the jury of the danger of convicting on the confession alone. Always seek independent confirmation of facts ad

People (DPP) v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 Facts — Doyle confessed to murder during his fifteenth interview. He argued his statements should be excluded because he had no solicitor present in the interview room. Held — By a 6:1 majority the Supreme Court held the constitutional right is a right of access to legal advice — not a right to have a solicitor physically present during questioning. The confession was not excluded on that ground. In the box — Access before and between interviews is the constitutional minimum; presence in the room is governed by current Garda policy, not by a constitutional right. CASE IN BRIEF WHERE THE LAW HAS MOVED. The position on solicitors has shifted in stages:

d: DPP v Ducque FACTS: Ducque charged with s.15 [2005] IECCA 92 sale/supply; argued mere possession.

s.t: B.2 The People (DPP) v Ducque [2005] IECCA 92 Citation: [2005] IECCA 92 (Court of Criminal Appeal). Facts: Sentence appeal in a s.15A case. Court considered the framework for assessing exceptional and specific circumstances. Holding: Exceptional and specific circumstances are case-specific. The court considers: the role of the offender (courier / facilitator / principal); plea (timing and value); cooperation (material assistance); personal circumstances (addiction, family); the nature and quantity of drugs; aggravating or mitigating context. Significance: Established the multi-factor framework for the exception analysis. The court's analysis is structured but the determination is fact-sensitive. Operational Application: Build the file so that the court has the material to conduct the Ducque analysis - the offender's role and circumstances are documented in the antecedent statement and investigation file. B.3

DPP v Dunne [1994] 2 IR 537 DPP v Foley [1995] 1 IR 267 Search warrant under Misuse of Drugs Act — strict compliance required. Possession — control + knowledge required. Constructive possession possible. Joint possession — multiple persons can be in joint control of drugs. S.15A MDA — value €13,000+ threshold; presumptive 10-year minimum subject to mitigation. S.15 sale/supply — mere possession of quantity can support inference of intent to supply. Sentencing — early plea, cooperation, no priors are key mitigators. Read warrant on entry. Note time of entry/exit. Question on knowledge of what was held. Surrounding context (paraphernalia, cash). Document each person's proximity, awareness, conduct on entry. Accurate Forensic Science Ireland valuation essential.

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Pringle (basic caution); DPP v Connolly (inference warnings); Heaney v Ireland. Dunne v Clinton [1930] IR 366 FACTS: An early constitutional case on the nature of arrest. The Supreme Court considered the constitutional foundation for any deprivation of liberty by Gardaí. PRINCIPLE: Every arrest must be grounded in either a common law power (e.g. breach of the peace) or statutory authority. There is no general power to detain for questioning, for investigation, or 'to help with enquiries'. The Garda must be able to point to the legal authority for the act of arrest. RATIO: Article 40.4.1 ('no citizen shall be deprived of liberty save in accordance with law') requires specific legal authority for every detention. 'Helping with enquiries' is not detention; if a person is not free to leave, that is arrest, and arrest n

Court testimony: 'I detained the suspect under section [x] of the [Act] on grounds of [stated grounds].' Habeas corpus is the remedy. Article 40.4.2 Bunreacht na hÉireann Habeas corpus FACTS: Foundational right to immediate judicial inquiry into lawfulness of detention. PRINCIPLE: High Court may inquire into lawfulness of any detention and order release where unlawful. PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If habeas corpus threatened, file must be presentable in minutes. Custody record, authorisations, briefings — all to hand. Weak foundation: charge or release before application is heard. CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 5(4) ECHR; Re Ó Laighléis. Operates where the accused had genuine selfdefence belief but used disproportionate force. s.t: A.3 Self-Defence The People (AG) v Dwyer [1972] IR 416 — Foundational Self-Defence Framework Citation: [1972] IR 416 (Court of Criminal Appeal / Supreme Court). Facts: The accused was convicted of the murder of a man during a confrontation in which the deceased had assaulted the accused. The accused responded with force that the trial court found exceeded the bounds of reasonable self-defence. Holding: Self-defence in Irish law has two limbs: (a) the accused must have honestly believed in the need to use force, and (b) the force used must have been reasonable in the circumstances as the accused believed them to be. Where the accused honestly believed in the need to use force but used unreasonable force, the partial defence of “excessive self-defence” operates to reduce murder to manslaughter (but does not provide a complete acquittal). Reasoning: The court constructed a hybrid test — subjective on the question of belief, but objective (

CROSS-REFERENCES: s.6 CJA 1984; production orders under CJA 2011; DPP v Egan; Riley v California (US analogous reasoning). 2.7 Recurring Pitfalls in Search Powers Cumulative practice across courts shows the following recurring problems sinking search-based cases. Each is preventable. Wrong premises identified (recurring theme) FACTS: Warrant for No. 7 Flat A; executed at No. 7 Flat B. Or warrant for an apartment in a multiunit building where the warrant doesn't specify the unit. PRINCIPLE: Premises specificity is constitutional. Wrong premises = unlawful entry = evidence at risk. PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Read the warrant. Confirm the address with what you can see. If there's any doubt, pause and resolve before entry. Flats in inner-city Dublin often share house numbers — name the flat / unit / floor specifically in the warrant application. Gene

Inner / outer cordons. Photograph sequence: overall, mid-range, close-up. Sketch with measurements. Exhibits photographed in situ before recovery. Each Garda entering logged. CROSS-REFERENCES: Scene preservation protocols. DPP v Egan (Mobile phones) [2010] IECCA FACTS: Mobile phone evidence; question of search, examination, content extraction. PRINCIPLE: Phone seizure on arrest permissible under s.6 CJA 1984. Examination of content requires further authority (warrant or specific statutory power). Phones attract heightened protection — volume and intimacy of data. RATIO: Phones are not pockets — separate authorisation regime for content. PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Power off if possible. Faraday bag during transport. Do NOT browse content at station. Submit to Cyber Crime Unit / Computer Crime Unit with warrant or production order. PIN/passcode request under caution — refusal noted. CCU produces forensic image; analysis follows. COMMON PITFAL

Content extraction documented from CCU. COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Phone seized but not promptly to CCU. (2) Cell-site relied on without expert. (3) Authentication thin. CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Egan; CJA 2011 production orders. 4.5 Hearsay and Statutory Exceptions Hearsay Rule — Overview Common law + statutory exceptions FACTS: Out-of-court statement tendered for truth of contents is hearsay; presumptively inadmissible unless exception applies. PRINCIPLE: Hearsay rule preserves cross-examination as test of evidence. Exceptions: dying declarations, statements against interest, res gestae, business records, statutory exceptions (s.16 CJA 2006 etc), confessions (against maker), expert opinion. TEST: Is the statement hearsay? If yes, does an exception apply? PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Direct evidence preferable. Where hearsay arises, identify the exception. PULSE entries, official documents, business records — various exceptions. CROSS-REFERENCES: ss

s.t: D.6 Frumkin v Russia (2016) Application No. 74568/12 Citation: Application No. 74568/12, judgment 5 January 2016. Facts: Applicant attended a major protest in Moscow which descended into clashes between protesters and police. Mass arrests followed. Issue: state's handling of the assembly and the absence of dialogue with organisers. Holding: Violation of Article 11 (assembly). The authorities failed to maintain communication with the organisers and missed opportunities for dialogue and de-escalation. The state has a positive obligation to facilitate peaceable assembly including by constructive engagement with organisers. Reasoning: The Court emphasised that policing of mass assemblies is not merely a passive containment function. Active engagement, dialogue, accommodation are part of the Article 11 obligation. Significance: Positive obligation to engage. Failure to dialogue may itself constitute violation. Operational Application: Garda

Statements obtained under compulsion in a non-criminal investigation may not subsequently be used as evidence at a criminal trial. Why It Matters: Be cautious about evidence trail from regulatory or civil inquiries (Revenue, financial regulators, employer audits). s.t: Conscious denial or frustration of reasonable access to a solicitor renders the detention unlawful and resulting admissions liable to exclusion. The right of reasonable access is a constitutional right. People (DPP) v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 Holding / principle. A suspect who has requested legal advice must not be questioned until that advice has been obtained; evidence (including forensic samples in White) taken in breach is liable to exclusion. The right to legal advice attaches before and around questioning, not merely on paper.

People (DPP) v Gormley and White Ratio. A suspect who has asked for legal advice must not be questioned, nor samples taken, until that advice is obtained; breach exposes the material to exclusion. Apply it. No interview questions until the solicitor consultation has actually happened. Full treatment: p.849

B.8.1 Constitutional rights — liberty and access to a solicitor People (DPP) v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 Facts — Gormley asked for a solicitor on arrest, but was interviewed before the solicitor arrived and made admissions that were used to convict him. (In the joined case, White was required to give forensic samples before legal advice.) Held — Basic fairness applies from the moment of arrest. A suspect who has requested legal advice must be given a reasonable opportunity to get it before questioning begins; questioning should be deferred until then. Gormley’s admissions were excluded and his conviction quashed. (White: there was no equivalent right to advice before giving compelled, objective forensic samples.) In the box — If a suspect has asked for a solicitor, do not begin or continue questioning until they have had access to legal advice. This is the law behind A.4. CASE IN BRIEF

d: People (DPP) v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 Holding / principle.

1.1 Theft from Person Theft from the person: theft where the property is taken from the body or immediate presence of the injured party (mugging, bag-snatch, pickpocketing, distraction theft) — contrary to s.4 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. Distinction from robbery: if force is used on the person or the threat of force is used immediately before or at the time of the taking, the offence is robbery (s.14), not theft. The line can be fine — a pickpocket who is detected and pushes the victim away to escape may cross into robbery (R v Hale, appropriation as continuing act). Legal Framework All elements of theft (1.0 above) apply. Additionally: • Use of force converts the offence to robbery (s.14) — see Chapter 3. • Where the IP is targeted because of vulnerability (intoxicated, elderly, asleep), this is an aggravating factor for sentence. • Theft from person frequently engages identification evidence — ADVOKATE assess

Facts: Handyside, publisher of 'The Little Red Schoolbook', was prosecuted under the Obscene Publications Act. He claimed breach of Article 10 (freedom of expression). Holding: Freedom of expression under Article 10 is one of the essential foundations of a democratic society. It applies not only to information or ideas that are favourably received or regarded as inoffensive but also to those that offend, shock, or disturb the State or any sector of the population. Restrictions must be: prescribed by law; pursue a legitimate aim; be necessary in a democratic society. Reasoning: The Court's analysis: 'such are the demands of that pluralism, tolerance and broadmindedness without which there is no democratic society'. The proportionality assessment is the operative test for

WHY IT MATTERS: Authority extends only to persons on premises at execution. HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence challenges the search; evidence may be excluded. HOW TO PREVENT: Search persons on premises only. Persons outside need separate grounds (s.23 MDA, s.30 CJ(PO)A, s.8 CJA 2006). s.t: B.3 The People (DPP) v Heffernan [2017] IESC 5 - Sustained Provocation Citation: [2017] IESC 5 (Supreme Court). Facts: Heffernan killed his wife in circumstances that involved a sustained pattern of conflict, mental health issues, and an immediate precipitating event. The defence raised provocation including the cumulative impact of the relationship dynamics. Holding: Provocation in Irish law accommodates cumulative or sustained provocation, not only immediate provocation. The killer's state of mind at the time of the killing is the inquiry; the contribution of sustained prior provocation is relevant. Reasoning: The Supreme Court extended the MacEoin / Davis subjective framework to accommodate cumulative provocation. The immediate event may be the trigger; the accumulated abuse / pressure is part of the subjective state-of-mind picture. Significance: Important development for the DV-homicide scenario where the killer is the long-term IP

d: B.3 The People (DPP) v Heffernan [2017] IESC 5 - Sustained Provocation Citation: [2017] IESC 5 (Supreme Court).

A defendant who induced a vulnerable person to make 'gifts' was held to have appropriated the property dishonestly. Relevant to theft-by-deception and elder-abuse cases.

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Lynch; DPP v Ward; DPP v Hoey. DPP v Hoey [1987] IR 637 FACTS: Allegations of inducement during interview; statement challenged. PRINCIPLE: Where any inducement is alleged with credibility, the burden is on prosecution to disprove it. Voluntariness is a prosecution burden. RATIO: Allegations of inducement, once raised credibly, must be disproved. TEST: Has the prosecution disproved the alleged inducement on the balance of probabilities? HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Recording and contemporaneous notes are the answer. If the inducement is not on the recording and the suspect alleges it, the recording is the prosecution's case. PRINCIPLE: Excessive interview duration without proper breaks producing exhaustion is oppression. The resulting confession is inadmissible. RATIO: Duration + lack of rest + cumulative pressure = oppression. s.t: Key Authority DPP v Hughes [2012] IECCA 69 — Force Element Holding: Force must be more than minimal contact incidental to a snatch. The grade of force required is a matter of fact for the jury; pushing, jerking, striking, threatening with a weapon all qualify. Why It Matters: Snatch theft is a frequent classification problem. If contact is purely incidental (bag-snap that catches the IP’s arm), it may be theft from person. If the suspect pulls the IP forward, knocks them off-balance, or pushes them, it is robbery.

Joint enterprise — multiple suspects, defence carves up roles to fit each into lesser offence. 187. Mens rea — “My client thought property belonged to him / he was reclaiming a debt.” Claim of right under s.2(1). Appellate Authority Where Defence Won • DPP v Hughes [2012] IECCA 69 — force element scrutinised; minimal-contact convictions vulnerable. •

d: Key Authority DPP v Hughes [2012] IECCA 69 — Force Element Holding: Force must be more than minimal contact incidental to a snatch.

s.t: Key Authority DPP v Hull [1996] IECCA — Inferred Intent Holding: Intent to kill or cause serious injury may be inferred from the nature of the act — weapon used, target area, force, repetition. Why It Matters: Build the inferential picture: weapon, target, repetition, force, suspect words / conduct before and after.

A.3 The Inferential Picture Beyond the formal burdens, several evidential inferences operate in practice: • S.4(2) CJA 1964 — the accused is presumed to have intended the natural and probable consequences of his conduct (rebuttable). • S.18 / 19 / 19A CJA 1984 (as amended) — adverse inferences from silence on specified matters, prerequisite-conditional. • DPP v Hull — intent may be inferred from the nature of the act, weapon, target, force, words, conduct. A.4 Practical Implications for the Investigator Understanding burdens tells the investigator what evidence to produce: • Build the prosecution case on every element, including mens rea. • Anticipate every defence likely to be raised — and produce evidence that will allow the prosecution to disprove it. • Preserve mental-health material in any case where insanity / diminished responsibility might enter (homicide especially). Murray is the touchstone Irish authority on the subjective standard of recklessness. It frames mens rea analysis across the NFOAP suite (s.2, s.3, s.4 assault; s.13 endangerment), and the Cagney and McGrath reaffirmation of 2008 keeps Murray live. Operational Application: Whenever recklessness is the live mens rea — typically in s.3 / s.4 NFOAP and s.13 endangerment — investigation should produce evidence of the accused’s subjective awareness of risk: their words at the time, their conduct showing knowledge of the situation, their experience or training that would put them on notice, their post-incident conduct showing awareness. s.t: Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017] UKSC 67 Ghosh modified — second subjective limb removed; dishonesty assessed objectively against ordinary standards. Persuasive in Ireland; defence counsel will cite both Ghosh and Ivey — know which the court is applying.

European and Comparative Authority R v Ghosh [1982] QB 1053 (CA, England) Test: The classic two-limb dishonesty test — (1) was the conduct dishonest by the ordinary standards of reasonable and honest people; (2) did the defendant realise it would be regarded as dishonest by those standards. Persuasive in Ireland and frequently cited. Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017] UKSC 67 Test: The UK Supreme Court modified Ghosh — the subjective second limb is removed. Dishonesty is now assessed objectively against ordinary standards, with reference only to what the defendant actually knew or believed about the facts. Defence counsel in Ireland will cite both; know which the court is applying.

d: Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017] UKSC 67 Test: The UK Supreme Court modified Ghosh — the subjective second limb is removed.

why: THE modern exclusionary rule. Replaced Kenny: evidence from unconstitutional breach admissible if breach was inadvertent — deliberate/conscious violations still excluded. Know it for every search/arrest challenge.

s.t: 10.2 The People (DPP) v JC [2015] IESC 31 - The Modern Exclusionary Rule The Principle in Operational Terms Evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights is liable to exclusion, subject to a narrowed exception where the breach was inadvertent and the prosecution can demonstrate it. The 'inadvertent' standard is genuine and not a saving formula - the test will be searching. The post-JC reality is procedural rigour at every step, because any breach risks the evidence. How the Defence Will Attack Identifying any procedural irregularity - a delay in caution, a slip in the rights notification, a gap in the chain and pressing for exclusion. 'You didn't follow the standard procedure on [step], did you?' 'You knew the procedure required X - and you chose to do Y?' 'If we accept this was a breach of my client's rights, the court is being asked to exclude the evidence.' How You Prepare Procedure followed at every step - and recorded contem

(3) Application made by a member not directly involved in investigation (better to send the actual investigator). CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.5; DPP v Dunne (warrant face validity); People (DPP) v JC (constitutional breach test); s.29 OASA 1939 (as amended post-Damache).

It is the defence’s primary route to strike out the product of an investigation, so the legal basis of every search, arrest and detention must be sound and recorded. People (DPP) v JC [2015] IESC 31 Holding / principle. The modern test for unconstitutionally obtained evidence. Automatic exclusion (the old Kenny rule) was replaced. Step one: was there a breach of a constitutional right? Step two: the prosecution may seek to justify admission — where the breach was inadvertent or the product of a genuine, non-culpable error, the court has a discretion to admit; where the breach was deliberate and conscious, reckless, or grossly negligent, the evidence is presumptively excluded. The prosecution bears the onus of establishing the basis for admission. Operational point. The defect that decides the case is the one created during the investigation. Get warrants right, record the legal basis and the grounds for every search and arrest contempor

d: People (DPP) v JC [2015] IESC 31 Holding / principle.

(3) Are circumstances of recognition described (lighting, angle, duration of viewing)? DPP v JEM [2001] FACTS: Bad character evidence admitted at trial; appeal. PRINCIPLE: The SC 4 IR 385 (SC) confirmed the general rule that bad character evidence is inadmissible — prior convictions, prior wrongful conduct not directly relevant. Narrow exceptions: (a) similar fact (where probative value outweighs prejudice), (b) rebuttal of good character put forward, (c) crossexamination on credibility where defendant attacks prosecution witnesses, (d) statutory gateways (e.g. coercive control pattern). RATIO: Bad character carries high prejudice; admissibility tightly controlled. TEST: Is there a specific gateway? Does probative value outweigh prejudicial effect?

d: DPP v JEM [2001] FACTS: Bad character evidence admitted at trial; appeal.

'It will go easier if you tell us', 'we can put in a good word', 'the judge will look favourably' — all inducement. RATIO: Inducement need not be express to vitiate voluntariness. TEST: Was any expression made that could reasonably be understood as conditional advantage? FACTS: Interview not properly recorded; statement challenged. RATIO: Recording is a protection both ways — protects suspect against fabrication, protects Gar

DPP v Kelly (with Butler and Doyle) case stated on ss.8 and 9 CJ(PO)A 1994 — verify neutral citation before citing Holding / principle. Charges under s.8 (failure to comply with a direction) and s.9 (wilful prevention or interruption of the free passage of a person or vehicle, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse) arising from a protest-type obstruction. The case turned on (a) whether the statutory prerequisite to a valid s.8 direction had been complied with, and (b) whether there was adequate evidence that the defendants acted “without lawful authority or reasonable excuse.” The charges were dismissed for evidential insufficiency on those elements. Operational point. Two lessons. For s.8, the direction is only as good as its statutory foundation — the member must have the required suspicion on reasonable grounds, the direction must be clear and lawful, and all of that must be in evidence. s.t: The ECHR subsequently upheld the privacy right of Norris as an expression of his individual personality and Irish law was ultimately changed.71 Privacy and Surveillance: Kennedy v Ireland In Kennedy v. Ireland,72 the first two Plaintiffs were journalists (Geraldine Kennedy and Bruce Arnold) whose phones had been tapped on foot of a warrant issued by the Minister for Justice. Conversations were taped and transcripts made although it was later admitted that there was no justification for this. The Plaintiffs claimed damages for breach of their constitutional rights. Hamilton P (as he then was) accepted that the Constitution guaranteed an unenumerated and fundamental personal right to privacy which flowed from the Christian and democratic nature of the State. He continued: “The dignity and freedom of an individual in a democratic society cannot be ensured if his communications of a private nature [...] are deliberately, consciously and unj

However, the crucial problem in this jurisdiction is the absence of regulation of other forms of electronic surveillance. Apart from general principles that can be derived from Kennedy v. Ireland the issue of surveillance can be seen in its most detailed form in the jurisprudence of the Convention. Business Telephone Tapping It is quite clear from the case law of the convention that calls from an office as well as a home phone fall within the ambit of privacy protection.

s.t: Document all interactions. DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 — Modern Exclusionary Rule Citation: [2015] IESC 31 (Supreme Court). Facts: The case concerned evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights and the question of admissibility. The Supreme Court considered whether the strict exclusionary rule of People (DPP) v Kenny [1990] 2 IR 110 should be modified. Holding: The court modified the Kenny rule. Where evidence is obtained in breach of constitutional rights, it is presumptively inadmissible, but the breach may be excused where it was inadvertent and excusable. Deliberate or conscious violation results in exclusion; inadvertent breach by an officer acting in good faith may not. Reasoning: The court balanced the deterrent / rights-protective function of exclusion against the cost of excluding probative evidence in cases of accidental breach. The good-faith exception is constrained — it does not licence carelessness. Significance: JC i

C.6 DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 Citation: [2015] IESC 31 (Supreme Court). Facts: JC's home was searched under an invalid warrant (the form of warrant was constitutionally defective post-Damache). Evidence was found. Issue on appeal: must the evidence be excluded? Holding: The strict exclusionary rule from People (DPP) v Kenny [1990] is modified. Evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights may be admitted where the breach was inadvertent and not the result of conscious and deliberate disregard or systemic carelessness. The court conducts a balancing exercise. Reasoning: Clarke J (delivering the majority judgment) framed a 'good faith' or inadvertence exception to the Kenny rule. Deliberate or systemically careless breaches still attract exclusion; inadvertent and good-faith breaches may be admitted in the balance of justice. Significance: The modern exclusionary rule. Procedural rigor remains essential - the JC margin is narrow. Del

Question: should evidence judge bench) obtained on the back of the invalid warrant be excluded? PRINCIPLE: A 4-3 majority overruled the absolute exclusionary rule in DPP v Kenny [1990]. The new test: evidence obtained in breach of a constitutional right is presumptively inadmissible. The prosecution may rebut by proving the breach was inadvertent (not deliberate, not conscious and intentional, not in reckless disregard). Deliberate or reckless breaches still exclude. The court will also consider: gravity of the breach, gravity of the offence, whether evidence would have been obtained anyway. RATIO: Inadvertent breaches may be excused; deliberate or reckless breaches will not be. TEST: (1) Was there a breach? (2) Was it inadvertent? (Burden on prosecution.) (3) Should the court exclude as a matter of discretion?

why: The old strict exclusionary rule (1990-2015): any breach of constitutional rights excluded evidence regardless of Garda good faith. Overruled by JC — but explains three decades of case law.

The Supreme Court reconsidered Kenny v DPP [1990] which had established an absolute exclusionary rule. PRINCIPLE: Evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights is presumptively inadmissible. Prosecution may rebut by establishing the breach was INADVERTENT — Garda acted in good faith without recognition of the breach. Deliberate or reckless breaches still result in exclusion. Onus is on prosecution to establish inadvertence. RATIO: Inadvertent breach + good faith may save evidence; deliberate or reckless breach excludes it. The functional equivalent of interrogation by state agents without warning or legal access violates the privilege against self-incrimination. Why It Matters: Be cautious about communications with detained suspects through any state-facing channel — including civilian volunteers, informants, even other Gardaí in informal settings. Anything elicited may be excluded. Khan v UK (Application 35394/97) 12 May 2000 — Covert Surveillance and Privacy Facts: Covert listening device used to record applicant; product used in evidence. Holding: Article 8 (private life) violated where surveillance was not adequately governed by domestic law. However, admission of the evidence did not per se violate Article 6 — trial fairness assessed in the round. Why It Matters: Covert surveillance must be done strictly within the Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 framework. Authorisation, recording, and chain are essential.

s.t: The same facts will frequently support either charge, and the choice is often a DPP direction — in one case the DPP withdrew a s.17 handling charge and substituted a s.18 possession charge on the same facts (a stolen car key). People (AG) v Lawless Court of Criminal Appeal Holding. On a receiving/possession charge it is not necessary to prove physical possession; it suffices to show the property was under the accused’s control. The court approved a direction to that effect. Operational point. Possession is knowledge plus control — a stash, a press, a car boot, or goods held through another can all be “possession.” Evidence the control, not just proximity. Possession therefore means knowledge plus control, and may be constructive or joint (see the Rules of Evidence chapter, §1 and the Drugs Supplement §1 for the same possession analysis). Mere physical custody without the guilty mind is not enough; nor is mere presence near the goods. Pe

s.t: B.3 The People (DPP) v Long [2009] 3 IR 486 Citation: [2009] 3 IR 486; [2008] IECCA 133 (Court of Criminal Appeal). Holding: Refined the s.15A sentencing approach. The court should identify the appropriate sentence by reference to the gravity of the offence (Tiernan structured discretion), and then apply the statutory minimum unless exception established. Where exception established, the court determines the appropriate departure. Significance: Brought the s.15A regime into the wider Tiernan structured discretion framework. The presumption is integrated with the general sentencing analysis. Operational Application: The file should support the Tiernan analysis (gravity, aggravation, mitigation) as well as the s.15A specific factors. The two analyses operate together. B.4

s.t: Of course, it may not be possible to hold an identification parade, or any other form of identification procedure, because of the refusal of the suspect to co-operate. In the course of The People (DPP) v Lynda Lee (CCA 20 July 2004) the Court acknowledged that the law relating to the frailty and undesirability of so-called ‘dock identification’ applies to the circumstances where a witness, who, prior to the events giving rise to the prosecution had no knowledge or acquaintance of an accused, identifies them for the first time by pointing them out in the Courtroom at the trial. The reasons why such an identification may be considered unsatisfactory are self evident since a witness may be inclined to make assumptions concerning the identity of the person who is sitting in a dock with a warder on either side. Hence the term ‘dock identification’. 6.17. Informal Identification Informal identification evidence may be admitted as evidence but

The Court held that this reason must be accounted less than satisfactory. In the case of The People (DPP) v Carroll (CCA ex tempore December 18, 2002), the Court of Criminal Appeal was unimpressed by the Garda’s explanation that it would have been difficult to arrange a parade of scruffy males in their 40s or 50s and it quashed convictions in a case where informal identification was the only evidence against the accused. The Garda had not explained why it would have been difficult to arrange a parade, such as that the accused was of singular appearance, and appeared not to have made any attempt to arrange a formal parade before recourse to informal means. In this case the accused was identified from a crowd by night. In the case of The People (DPP) v Lynda Lee (CCA, July 20, 2004) the appellant was identified informally in the District Court in the absence of any justification or explanation for not holding a formal parade. Subjective test — the accused’s actual response — but the loss of self-control must be sudden and temporary. A.2 Provocation — Partial Defence (Murder to Manslaughter) The People (DPP) v MacEoin [1978] IR 27 — Foundational Provocation Test Citation: [1978] IR 27 (Court of Criminal Appeal). Facts: The accused was convicted of the murder of a man with whom he had been drinking. On the evening of the killing, the accused alleged the deceased had attacked him with a hammer in a sustained way; the accused then took the hammer and struck the deceased multiple times, fatally. The trial judge directed the jury in conventional terms drawing on English authority — specifically the objective “reasonable man” test from English provocation jurisprudence. Holding: Provocation in Irish law is a partial defence reducing murder to manslaughter. The test is subjective — measured by the accused’s own response, not by a hypothetical reasonable person. The accused must have lost self-control, and the loss of control must have been sudden and temporary, not used as

Part B - Irish Authority Carrying to DV Context B.1 The People (DPP) v MacEoin [1978] IR 27 - Provocation Citation: [1978] IR 27 (Court of Criminal Appeal). Facts: MacEoin was convicted of murder. He claimed provocation - sustained verbal and physical aggression by the deceased culminating in the killing. The CCA considered the operative test for provocation in Irish law. Holding: Provocation in Irish criminal law is subjective. The accused must have actually lost self-control as a result of the provocation. The reasonable-person standard from English law (Camplin) is rejected as inconsistent with the subjective nature of the inquiry. Reasoning: Kenny J framed the test as a personal inquiry into the accused's state of mind. The accused's individual characteristics, sensitivities, and reactions are relevant - not the hypothetical reasonable person. Significance: Foundational Irish authority on subjective provocation. Distinguishes Irish

s.t: Part E — Custody, Interview, Admissibility The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 — Voluntariness of Admissions Citation: [1977] IR 336 (Supreme Court). Facts: The accused was convicted in part on the basis of admissions made during custody. The defence challenged the admissibility of those admissions on voluntariness grounds. Holding: Admissions are admissible only if voluntary — not obtained by oppression, threat, inducement, or other circumstance impugning free choice. The prosecution bears the burden of proving voluntariness beyond reasonable doubt. Significance: Madden is the foundational Irish authority on voluntariness. It anchors every admissibility challenge in custody-derived evidence. Operational Application: Every interview producing admissions must be supported by evidence of voluntariness: caution properly administered; suspect’s capacity assured; lawyer access vindicated; no oppression / inducement; rest, refreshment, me

B.7 The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 - Voluntariness of Statement Citation: [1977] IR 336 (Supreme Court). Significance: Foundational voluntariness for admissibility (treated in Volume 2). Operational Application (DV): In DV interviews - both of suspects and of IPs - voluntariness governs admissibility. The IP statement taken under pressure, in fear, or in the suspect's presence may be challenged on voluntariness grounds (rare; IP statements are not 'against' the IP but their reliability and admissibility can be tested). A.2 The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 - Voluntariness Operational Application (Drugs): Drug suspects in custody are frequently intoxicated, in withdrawal, or under stress. Where CCTV is the sole or primary source of identification and image quality is poor, conviction is unsafe without corroboration. Why It Matters: Improve CCTV evidence at source where possible — better cameras, multi-angle. Where image quality is fixed, build corroboration (clothing recovered, IMEI matching, suspect admissions on incidental facts). DPP v Maguire [2008] IECCA — Dock Identification Facts: Dock identification of accused by IP without prior formal ID procedure. Holding: Dock identification is the weakest form of ID evidence. Where a formal ID procedure (parade or VIPER) could have been conducted and was not, dock ID should be approached with extreme caution. Why It Matters: Always offer a formal ID procedure where the witness has not previously identified the suspect. Document the offer and any refusal.

Casey shapes investigation: identification procedures matter. Operational Application: Investigation involving visual identification: (a) document the conditions of the original sighting (lighting, distance, duration, angle); (b) where formal identification procedures are needed, use VIPER — Video Identification Parade Electronic Recording — rather than informal procedures; (c) ADVOKATE mnemonic for assessing the witness sighting: Amount of time, Distance, Visibility, Obstruction, Knowledge of suspect, Any reason to remember, Time lapse, Errors / discrepancies; (d) avoid leading procedures and contaminating subsequent witness accounts. s.t: Marks v Beyfus (1890) 25 QBD 494 Holding / principle. Informer privilege: the identity of an informant is protected in the public interest, so that the flow of information is not deterred. The exception is where disclosure is necessary to establish the accused’s innocence (“innocence at stake”), in which case the privilege must yield. Operational point. Keep the intelligence-to-evidence divide clean — the sterile corridor (see Volume 10). Source identity stays in the intelligence system; build the evidential case on independently obtainable material so source identity is never put in issue at trial. Public interest immunity (PII). Sensitive material — methods, premises, third-party safety — may be withheld where the public interest in non-disclosure outweighs the defence’s interest in disclosure. But the disclosure obligation to the defence is the default, and PII is decided by the court, not by the investigator. Flag sensitive material

s.t: 4.18. Confessions resulting from questioning The admissibility of confessions resulting from questioning was considered by the Court of Criminal Appeal in Attorney General v. Mc Cabe [1927] I.R. 129. The following passage has relevance. "Though this matter has been discussed with varying opinion for many years, it is somewhat difficult and perhaps not desirable, to extract a general positive rule from the judgments and text writers. It can be posited, however, that it is not the law that a statement must be excluded from evidence on the sole ground that either the statement was made in answer to questions put by a police officer or that it was made without a caution having been first administered. But in such cases it is a matter for the judge at the trial to decide whether, in his judicial discretion, he will admit the statement or not having regard to all the circumstances and observing the legal requirement that the statement shall b

It is not the law that the statement of an accused must be rejected if it be not in their actual words; it is, nevertheless, a matter for consideration by the trial Judge in arriving at a decision as to the admission or rejection of the statement - Attorney General v. McCabe [1927] I.R. 129. 4.29. Recording written statements When a suspect is willing to make a written statement, the rules listed at Chapter 3.19 for the recording of a statement of a witness should be observed. In the case of a suspect, a notebook may be used to record the statement, but only in exceptional circumstances. In the case of a statement made by an accused person, the caution should be written in the third person immediately after the heading as follows: "You are not obliged to say anything, etc.") When a particular stage has been reached at which it is considered necessary to administer the caution, the caution should be administered. s.t: The main distinction between the voluntariness of a statement and the Judges' Rules is that a Court must exclude a statement which is not voluntary, but in the case of a statement obtained in contravention of the Judges' Rules, the Court has a discretion to admit or not to admit it in evidence but can only admit it if it is voluntary. Mere non compliance with the Judges' Rules does not in itself render a statement inadmissible in law and that fact in itself does not indicate that the statement is not voluntary - McCarrick v. Leavy [1964] I.R. 225. Note re Caution: It is important to note that with regard to the Caution, the judges rules set out below, do not take account of s.19A of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 (as inserted by the CJA 2007) and other relevant provisions in relation to inferences from silence. If a person is cautioned according to the Judges Rules, the prosecution will not be able to rely on any inferences from the sile

Mc Carrick v. Leavy [1964] I.R. 225 In this case the complainant (a member of the Garda Síochána) was investigating a hit and run accident in which it was alleged that a motor car, of which the defendant (Leavy) was the registered owner, was involved. In the course of his evidence in the District Court (on charges under the Road Traffic Act, 1933, now repealed) the complainant stated that he interviewed the defendant and asked him who was driving the motor car at the time of the accident. The District Justice decided not to admit evidence of the answer to the question on the ground that as the defendant was not cautioned, it would not be a voluntary statement. In a case stated, the Supreme Court ruled that the District Justice had erred in holding that a failure to comply with the Judges' Rules in itself made the evidence involuntary and inadmissible. s.t: Document the accused’s own account of their level of control. Defence cases presenting the accused as merely angry, hurt, or jealous — without behavioural loss of self-control — fall short of Davis. DPP v McDonagh [2001] 3 IR 411 — Provocation and Trial Direction Citation: [2001] 3 IR 411 (Court of Criminal Appeal). Facts: The accused was convicted of murder. On appeal, issues arose as to the adequacy of the trial judge’s directions on provocation, including how the jury should approach the subjective test. Holding: Reaffirmed the MacEoin / Davis approach. Trial judges must direct juries clearly that the test is subjective — measured by the accused’s actual response — and must explain what loss of self-control means in the behavioural sense. Significance: McDonagh sits in the line of appellate cases requiring full and clear provocation directions whenever the defence is in play. Inadequate directions are a route to conviction-quashing.

McGreevy v DPP [1973] 1 All ER 503 (HL, on appeal from Northern Ireland) Holding. There is no special form of jury direction required in a circumstantial case. The ordinary direction — that the prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt — suffices; the judge is not obliged, as a separate rule of law, to direct that the facts must be inconsistent with any rational conclusion other than guilt. Operational point. The protection for the accused is the criminal standard itself, applied to the whole circumstantial picture — not a magic formula.

DPP v McMahon FACTS: Gardaí entered licensed premises [1986] IR 393 (SC) ostensibly for licensing inspection but with primary purpose of criminal investigation. PRINCIPLE: Henchy J held that lawful entry whose purpose is then abused renders the entry unlawful from the outset — the doctrine of trespass ab initio. RATIO: A power exercised for an ulterior purpose is no power at all. Pretextual entries are unlawful. TEST: What was the dominant purpose of entry? If the stated power was a cover for an investigation that did not yet have its own lawful basis — trespass.

Facts: Case involving an assault in which force was applied indirectly — not by direct blow but by mechanism. Holding: Force in s.2 NFOAP 1997 may be applied directly (a blow) or indirectly (setting an animal on a person, pulling a chair away as a person sits, throwing an object, contaminating food). The accused’s act need not be the immediate cause of the contact; an intermediate cause does not necessarily break the chain. Significance: Murphy expands the operational scope of assault. Key Authority DPP v Murray [1977] IR 360 — Recklessness Holding: Recklessness involves conscious disregard of an obvious and serious risk. The accused must have actually adverted to the risk; objective recklessness alone is insufficient. Why It Matters: Interview must probe what the accused was aware of — not what a reasonable person should have known. “Did you realise there was a risk that…?”

s.t: Part A — Homicide Authority (Irish) A.1 Mens Rea and Intent The People (DPP) v Murray [1977] IR 360 — Subjective Recklessness Citation: [1977] IR 360 (Supreme Court). Facts: The Murray case concerned the killing of a Garda. The accused fired a weapon during the course of the offence. The question for the court was the level of mens rea required for the capital offence of murder of a member of An Garda Síochána, in particular whether constructive or objective recklessness was sufficient or whether subjective awareness was required. Holding: Recklessness in Irish criminal law is subjective. The accused must have been actually aware of the risk — not merely whether a reasonable person would have been aware. Constructive recklessness, where the accused did not personally appreciate the risk but ought to have done so, does not meet the Irish criminal-law threshold. Reasoning: The Supreme Court drew the subjective test from the moral foundati

Part A - Irish Authority on Mens Rea (Carries to POA Offences) A.1 The People (DPP) v Murray [1977] IR 360 Citation: [1977] IR 360 (Supreme Court). Facts: Murray was charged with capital murder of a Garda following a shooting during a bank robbery. The case turned on the mens rea of capital murder under the (then) Criminal Justice Act 1964 - specifically whether knowledge that the victim was a Garda was required. Holding: Recklessness is a subjective concept in Irish criminal law. An accused is reckless if he or she was aware of a risk and proceeded to take it. The standard is not what a reasonable person would have foreseen but what this accused actually foresaw. Reasoning: The Supreme Court (Henchy, Griffin and Parke JJ in particular) rejected an objective recklessness test as inconsistent with the principle that the prosecution must prove the accused's actual state of mind. Henchy J's analysis is foundational: "a person is reckless i

The People (DPP) v Nevin [2003] 3 IR 321 Holding. A wholly circumstantial murder conviction (the conviction of Catherine Nevin for the murder of her husband) upheld by the Court of Criminal Appeal. A central ground concerned the trial judge’s treatment of the alternative innocent explanations the defence had advanced for the circumstantial evidence — illustrating that the live battleground in a circumstantial case is the competing innocent hypotheses. Operational point. Anticipate and, on the evidence, close off the innocent explanations the defence will advance — before charge, not at trial. s.t: AG,69 the SC (4:1 majority) identified the unenumerated right to marital privacy guaranteed by Article 40.3.1, which included a right to decide to use contraceptives Norris v AG/the Recognition of Pure Privacy In Norris v. AG70 the right to privacy per se was argued as rendering unconstitutional the criminalisation of certain homosexual conduct between consenting adult males. The argument failed on the facts with a majority of the court emphasising the Christian nature of the state, the obligation of the state to discourage conduct that was morally wrong, the effect of marriage and the spread of venereal diseases. Nonetheless, all judges either explicitly or implicitly recognised the privacy right though emphasising such considerations as State security, public order or morality, or other essential components of the common good would justify a restriction of the right.
