CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Cooney; Braddish v DPP (active preservation); DPP v O'Carroll. 4.3 Forensic Evidence and Chain of Custody DPP v Bowes [2004] 4 IR 223 (CCA) FACTS: Drugs prosecution; chain of custody for seized substance challenged. PRINCIPLE: Chain of custody must be unbroken from seizure to laboratory to court. Each handler is a link. Break may render evidence inadmissible. RATIO: Continuity is foundational to forensic reliability. TEST: Can the prosecution prove the item examined and produced in court is the item seized? HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence examines: who seized? Who transported? Who logged in property? Who transferred to FSI? Who at FSI received? Each link contested. SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On [date] at [time] I seized [item] from [location] in [premises]. I sealed it in tamper-evident exhibit bag [number] and si

Without it, CCTV at risk. HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence demands authentication; thin authentication exposes evidence. HOW TO PREVENT: Statement from keeper covering: system make/model, recording method, time-stamp accuracy method, retention, access. Master copy preserved. CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien; DPP v O'Carroll. ✗ Recognition Without Foundation DPP v Cooney / Casey THE ISSUE: 'I recognised the accused on CCTV' without articulating prior familiarity. WHY IT MATTERS: Recognition admissibility requires foundation of familiarity established by witness's own evidence. HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence: 'On what basis do you recognise the accused?' Vague answers undermine the identification. HOW TO PREVENT: Statement structure: foundation first (length of acquaintance, nature of contacts, identifying features), then recognition act, then basis (specific features visible on footage). s.t: Time gap between theft and possession is critical — the shorter the gap, the stronger the inference. Operational application: Document the time gap between reported theft and discovery of suspect's possession. Question the suspect on provenance under caution. Any answers — 'I bought it', 'I found it', 'someone gave it to me' — must be verbatim and tested. Trace the alleged source. DPP v O'Loughlin [1979] IR 85 Facts: O'Loughlin was found in possession of stolen property and charged with handling. The mens rea — knowledge or belief that the goods were stolen — was contested. Held: Mens rea for handling requires knowledge or belief that the goods were stolen, not mere suspicion. Wilful blindness, however, will satisfy the test — a deliberate refusal to enquire when circumstances called for enquiry. Ratio: Knowledge or belief that goods are stolen is required for handling; wilful blindness is included. Suspicion alone is not enough. Operat

Critical for the UTMV-to-theft upgrade. If the suspect’s dealings with the vehicle effectively eliminate the owner’s use or value, theft under s.4 may be the right charge. DPP v O’Loughlin [1979] IR 85 — “Taking” Holding: Taking requires asportation — actual movement of the vehicle. Simply sitting in it is not enough; the vehicle must be moved. Why It Matters: Where suspect found inside vehicle but engine off, key not turned, no movement — charge fits more naturally as attempt or interference, not the substantive UTMV. How to Bullet-Proof • Owner statement explicit on non-consent and authorised driver list. • CCTV / ANPR placing the suspect as driver (not just passenger). • Forensic on driver-position controls (steering, gear, handbrake). • Interview probes knowledge for any passenger. s.t: A.4 Branko Tomasic and Others v Croatia (2009) Application No. 46598/06 Citation: Application No. 46598/06, judgment 15 January 2009. Facts: The applicant's daughter and granddaughter were killed by the daughter's partner, who had been convicted of threats to kill them and had been ordered to undergo psychiatric treatment - but the treatment had not been provided. He killed them and himself shortly after release. Holding: Violation of Article 2. The authorities had specific knowledge of the threat and had failed to take the steps that the court orders required (provision of psychiatric treatment), with foreseeable lethal consequences. Reasoning: The Court applied the Osman test. The threat was specific and known; the State response was inadequate (failure of statutory psychiatric treatment); the failure was causally connected to the deaths. Significance: Extends the positive obligation to require effective implementation of protective m

s.t: D.3 Bukta and Others v Hungary (2007) Application No. 25691/04 Citation: Application No. 25691/04, judgment 17 July 2007. Facts: Applicants attended a spontaneous demonstration in response to a sudden political event. Police dispersed the assembly because notification had not been given as required by domestic law. Holding: Dispersal of a spontaneous peaceable assembly solely for failure to give prior notification breaches Article 11. Where the assembly is a response to a current event and prior notification was not practicable, the right to peaceful assembly attaches. Reasoning: The Court emphasised that strict application of notification requirements to spontaneous assembly defeats the purpose of the right. Assemblies responding to immediate events must be accommodated where peaceable. Significance: Spontaneous assembly protection. Operational implication for Irish public order policing of unplanned protests. Operational Application:

s.t: D.4 Kudrevicius and Others v Lithuania [GC] (2015) Application No. 37553/05 Citation: Application No. 37553/05, Grand Chamber judgment 15 October 2015. Facts: Farmers protested by blocking major roads for two days. Significant disruption to road users. Convicted of rioting under domestic law and sentenced to suspended imprisonment. Issue on Article 11. Holding: Article 11 protects peaceful assembly but the State has a wider margin where the assembly causes substantial disruption to public life. Conviction and sanction for sustained, deliberate, significantly disruptive blocking of major roads did not breach Article 11. Reasoning: The Grand Chamber emphasised the balance between assembly rights and the rights of others (public order, freedom of movement, economic activity). Significant deliberate disruption falls outside the most protected core of Article 11. Significance: Defines the limit of Article 11 protection in disruptive protest

Nature of Identification Parade As stated earlier there is neither legislation nor formal regulations covering the precise nature of an identification parade. We rely on court decisions for this. In the case of The People (DPP) v O’Reilly [1990] 2 IR 415 (CCA) and in the case of The People (DPP) v James O’Hanlon (CCA 12 October 1998) formal identification parades were held to involve the assembly of eight or nine people of similar age, height, appearance, dress and walk of life as the suspect. It was further held that the parade should be supervised by an independent Garda (that is, one not concerned with the actual investigation); that full details would be kept of the description of the various people making up the parade and that the witness should not have any opportunity of seeing the suspect in advance of the holding of the parade. s.t: In this instance the Gardai are entitled to obtain identification by less formal means. In the case of The People (Attorney General) v Martin [1956] IR 22 the suspect was identified from a makeshift identification parade that was sprung on the suspect while in custody in the Bridewell. The Supreme Court held that there is no rule of law or practice that requires visual identification of a person to be proved by means of an identification parade; each case must be considered on its own facts. It is readily understandable that other types of identification may in certain circumstances be fairer and more dependable than a formal identification parade which, because of its surroundings, atmosphere, range of choice and limited opportunity for observation, may be a less than satisfactory means of achieving a reliable identification. But the acceptability of an alternative method must always depend on the circumstances of the case. In the case

s.t: In fact, one of the major problems in the administration of justice is that posed by evidence of visual identification. The question of visual identification was considered by the Supreme Court in The People v. Casey (No. 2) [1963] I.R. 22. The following extract from the judgment delivered in that case indicates that approach of the Courts to the matter: "We are of opinion that juries as a whole may not be fully aware of the dangers involved in visual identification nor of the considerable number of cases in which such identification has been proved to be erroneous; and also that they may be inclined to attribute too much probative effect to the test of an identification parade. In our opinion it is desirable that in all cases where the verdict depends substantially on the correctness of an identification, their attention should be called in general terms to the fact that in a number of instances such identification has proved erroneous

s.t: A Court has no discretion to admit a confession or statement made by an accused person which is inadmissible because it was not made voluntarily. lf a Court decides that a confession was not voluntary, it must be excluded from the evidence - People v. Cummins [1972] I.R. 312. • Whether the confession was obtained in contravention of the Judges' Rules. If a Court determines that a confession was voluntary, then it will consider if it was obtained in accordance with the Judges' Rules. Insofar as admissions alleged to have been made by an accused person are tendered in evidence 2 Crime Investigation Techniques Manual

and were made or obtained in circumstances which are in contravention of the Judges' Rules, then the Court has a judicial discretion to accept or reject the admissions in question provided the Court is satisfied that they were voluntary - People v. Cummins (supra) If held to be admissible a confession is only evidence against a person making it. 4.6. Origin and status of Judges' Rules In 1912 the Judges, at the request of the Home Secretary, drew up some rules as a guide to police officers in the investigation of crime. These rules do not have the force of law; they are administrative directions, the observance of which the Police Authorities should enforce on their subordinates as tending to the fair administration of justice. It is important that they should do so, for statements obtained from persons contrary to the spirit of these Rules may be rejected as evidence by the judge presiding at the trial - R. v. Voisin [1918] 1 K.B. 531.

The People v. Cummins [1972] I.R. 312 In this case the accused when in custody on one offence was questioned by a member of the Garda about another wholly unconnected offence. There was no evidence to connect him with the other offence and the reason why the member questioned him was because of his experience and knowledge of the accused, and the nature of the offence. In reply, without any caution having been administered, the accused made a self incriminatory statement. On the trial for the second offence, the trial Judge ruled out the self incriminatory statement because it was not voluntary. In a case stated, the Supreme Court held that the trial Judge wrongly rejected the evidence on the ground that it was not voluntary, firstly, because the Trial Judge appeared to have assumed that evidence obtained in breach of the Judges' Rules is to be treated in the same way as a 6 Crime Investigation Techniques Manual

d: Similarly, if there is any element of compulsion, this will also lead to the exclusion of the evidence – People (AG) v Cummins (1972) I.R. 312.

s.t: The People v Farrell [1978] I.R. 13 In this case the Supreme Court was of the view that statements which have been obtained in breach of the Judges Rules will normally be excluded. Chief Justice O’Higgins stated as follows: “The Judges’ Rules are not rules of law. They are rules for the guidance of persons taking statements. However, they have stood up to the test of time and will be departed from at peril. In very rare cases, such as R. V Mills and Lemon [1947 K.B. 247 a statement taken in breach may be admitted in evidence but in very exceptional circumstances. Where, however, there is a breach of the Judges’ Rules, such as a failure to make a written record of the alleged confession or a failure to invite the accused to accept or reject the statements, each of such breaches calls for an adequate explanation. The breaches and the explanation (if any) together with the entire circumstances of the case are matters to be taken into consi

s.t: Improper Methods of Visual Identification The following methods of visual identification should not be adopted: • Direct confrontation: that is showing a suspect alone to a witness or otherwise pointing out the suspect to a witness and asking the witness if they can identify them. This method was used in The People v. Martin [1954] I.R. 22 in respect of two different accused persons. • Identification on Court appearance: that is when a suspect is actually appearing before the Court and a witness is asked for the first time to identify the accused as the person responsible. 6.7. Correct Visual Identification Procedures The visual identification of suspects may be established as follows: • By the use of photographs • By the use of videotape • By identification parade (formal or informal) • By the use of composite sketches. s.t: The employer would clearly be in authority in the case of a charge against their servant of embezzlement or arson of their property, but not against the servant on a charge of murdering their own child. A confession made to, but not induced by, a person in authority is admissible, while, conversely, a confession induced by, but not made to, such a person will be rejected. An inducement not held out by a person in authority but in their presence is the same as an inducement held out by a person in authority unless the person in authority disassociates themselves completely from the inducement - People v Murphy [1947] I.R. 236. 4.12. Inducement, threat or promise The use or threat of violence by a person in authority would plainly render a confession inadmissible. There are numerous recorded decisions showing that the vaguest hint of a threat or promise may be fatal in this respect. Crime Investigation Techniques Manual 7

Duration of an inducement Where an improper inducement has been held out by a person in authority, the Court must be satisfied that the inducement had ceased to operate on the mind of the accused, and that the statement was not made under the influence of that inducement, regardless of whether or not a caution is administered - The People v. Murphy (supra). In the People v. Pringle, 2 Frewen 57, the Court of Criminal Appeal held that the effect of an inducement on the accused had dissipated by the time he made his statement of admission because he had a consultation with his solicitor prior to making the statement. 4.14. Oppressive Questioning

"It would have been better if you had told at first" - R. v. Walkey (1833) 6 C. and P. 175. The expression "you are all right" meaning "you will be all right" constitutes an improper inducement - The People v. Murphy [1947] I.R. 236. The accused at a stage when he was a suspect asked a police officer: "If I make a statement will you give me bail now?" The police officer answered "Yes". The accused then made a statement and it was held to be inadmissible as it resulted from an inducement - R. v. Zaveckas (1970) 54 Cr. App. R. 202. The accused asked a police officer whether a number of crimes would be "taken into account" (rather than made the subject of separate charges). The police officer's reply was that the police would have no objection to such a course. The accused then made a confession and it was held to be inadmissible - R. v. Northam (1967) 52 Cr. App. R. 97. A member investigating the unlawful taking of a motor car invoked sta

The admissibility of a statement made by a suspect may ultimately depend on: • Whether the confession was voluntary • Whether or not the suspect was in lawful custody when they made it In the People (DPP) v Mc Cann [1988] 4 I.R. 397 the Court of Criminal Appeal quoting from the Supreme Court decision in the People v Shaw [1982] I.R. 1 stated that a mere desire to interrogate a person cannot be put forward as a justification for an arrest under Section 30 but once there is proper suspicion it is clear that a person may be interrogated after they have been arrested or even without being arrested at all. s.t: Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power Power of arrest: • s.4 Criminal Law Act 1997 — arrestable offence (theft is punishable by 5 years or more on indictment; s.4 applies). • Without warrant where reasonable suspicion is held by the arresting member personally (DPP v Walsh). • On arrest, state the offence in plain terms (Christie v Leachinsky / DPP v Pringle), administer caution, identify yourself, and convey to a Garda station. Where the suspect is detained at scene by store security or by a bystander, the detention is lawful only if reasonable; immediate Garda attendance is essential. Document the time of detention and the time of arrest separately. A prolonged civilian detention can ground a defence challenge under Article 5 ECHR. Interview Plan PEACE-aligned. Common Pitfalls • Accepting suspect's stated identity without verification — see Worked Example 2 above. • Failing to administer caution before any substantive question at scene (DPP v Pringle / DPP v Conroy). • Allowing prolonged civilian detention by store security before Garda arrival without documenting times. • Letting CCTV preservation slide past day 1. Treat day 0 as the trigger. • Conducting dock identification only — no parade, no VIPER — in a case where the IP's view was brief. • Failing to identify other potential offences: assault, robbery, criminal damage during the same incident. • Not photographing the scene before bystanders move — once disturbed it cannot be reconstructed. s.t: A detailed description should first be obtained from the witness as a means of recording the parameters of the witness’s memory prior to the inspection of the photographs The Court of Criminal Appeal in People (DPP) v Rapple [1999] 1 ILRM 113 affirmed Lord Hewart’s statement in R. v Melaney (1923) 18 Cr. App. R. 2 at 3 (CA) of the use of photographs only when “the person has not been arrested and the police are in the greatest doubt who is the culprit”. Photographic identification procedure then may be used only as a preliminary step in a situation where the identity of a culprit is unknown. Photographs should be used only where the following conditions exist: • The suspect has not been arrested • There are no prospects of effecting an early arrest • The identity of the suspect cannot otherwise be established The Court of Criminal in Rapple cited above at 117-118 distinguished between two situations in which photographs may be shown to

The Supreme Court held that there is no rule of law or practice that requires visual identification of a person to be proved by means of an identification parade; each case must be considered on its own facts. It is readily understandable that other types of identification may in certain circumstances be fairer and more dependable than a formal identification parade which, because of its surroundings, atmosphere, range of choice and limited opportunity for observation, may be a less than satisfactory means of achieving a reliable identification. But the acceptability of an alternative method must always depend on the circumstances of the case. In the case of The People (DPP) v Rapple [1999] 7 ILRM 113 (CCA) the witness was brought in a Garda car to see whether he could identify the suspect whom it was expected would be walking along a particular road, and the victim identified the appellant as his attacker. s.t: An inference is permissible only where: the suspect was told in ordinary, non-technical language what the failure might lead to; was given a reasonable opportunity to consult a solicitor before the relevant failure; and the questioning was electronically recorded (or the suspect consented in writing to it not being recorded). A failure on any safeguard defeats the inference. The regime’s constitutionality was upheld in Rock v Ireland [1997] 3 IR 484. Critically, an inference can corroborate but can never be the sole or main basis for a conviction. Operational note The inference provisions are tools to be deployed deliberately, with the correct statutory “request and explanation” on the recording, solicitor access secured first, and the precise objects/marks/place identified. A sloppy or generic invocation produces nothing usable. Treat them as a supplement to a case already built, never as a substitute for evidence. Working reference -

The inference regime survives because of its safeguards — legal advice, ordinary-language warning, recording, and the bar on conviction on an inference alone. Heaney v Ireland [1996] 1 IR 580 — the right to silence is not absolute and may be restricted by proportionate measures serving a pressing social need. Rock v Ireland [1997] 3 IR 484 — the Supreme Court upheld the inference provisions (then ss. 18–19) as a proportionate restriction on the right to silence, given the safeguards attaching to them. Murray (John) v United Kingdom (1996) 22 EHRR 29 (ECtHR) Facts — A suspect was questioned and inferences were later drawn from his silence under the Northern Ireland regime; he had been denied access to a solicitor at the start of detention. Held — Drawing inferences from silence is not in itself a breach of the right to a fair trial, provided proper safeguards exist and the inference is not the sole or main basis of conviction. But access

s.t: 3.1 Persuasive authority on “reasonable firmness” R v Sanchez (1996) — English; persuasive only Holding / principle. On affray, the violence relied on must be such as would cause a notional bystander of reasonable firmness to fear for their personal safety. Where the violence was confined to two individuals in an open outdoor space, with ample room for any bystander to withdraw, an affray conviction was quashed — there was no real risk to a person of reasonable firmness. Operational point. Useful for understanding the objective element common to all three offences: it is not enough that two people fought; the prosecution must show the conduct was such as would frighten a notional bystander of reasonable firmness. In the inner city the setting usually supplies this (a crowded street, projectiles, weapons) — but it must be articulated in the evidence, not assumed. English authority; not binding here. Working reference - not legal advice -

s.t: Underpins the Irish position in Doyle and Gormley. Why It Matters: Do not commence interview before the solicitor consultation has occurred (if requested). Document the chain meticulously. Murray (John) v UK (Application 18731/91) 8 February 1996 — Inferences from Silence Facts: Applicant relied on silence; inferences drawn at trial. Holding: Drawing inferences from silence is not automatically a violation of Article 6, provided (a) the conviction is not solely or mainly based on the inference and (b) the warning is properly given. Significance: Underpins the inference warning regime in s.18 / s.19 / s.19A of the CJA 1984 (as amended). Why It Matters: When invoking inference provisions, follow the precise statutory wording, ensure the warning is recorded, ensure the consultation right is satisfied. Imperfect warnings sink the inference. Saunders v UK (Application 19187/91) 17 December 1996 — Privilege Against Self-Incrimination Facts: C

DPP v Shaw [1982] IR 1 Detention purely for the purpose of questioning, without statutory authority, is unlawful imprisonment. Confessions obtained may be excluded. Never 'invite' someone to the station and hold them without arrest. Either they're free to leave or they're under arrest — there is no middle ground.

The case is consistently applied in Irish law. TEST: Was the substance of the offence communicated in terms the suspect could understand at or as soon as practicable after the arrest? DPP v Shaw [1982] FACTS: Shaw held for 44 hours after arrest for kidnap/murder, primarily for IR 1 (SC) interrogation, without specific statutory power authorising detention beyond a limited period. Statements were challenged. PRINCIPLE: Walsh J held that detention purely for questioning, without specific statutory authority, amounts to unlawful imprisonment. Griffin J introduced 'extraordinary excusing circumstances' as a possible saving of evidence — but the principle stands: no common law power to detain for questioning. RATIO: Either statutory power exists (s.4 CJA 1984, s.30 OASA 1939, s.50 CJA 2007, s.16 CJ(DT)A 1996) or the person must be released. The 'helping with enquiries' fiction is dead in Irish law. s.t: It is excluded if obtained by oppression, or by a threat or inducement held out by a person in authority, or in circumstances that sap the free exercise of the suspect’s will. Voluntariness is decided by the judge on the voir dire and the prosecution bears the onus of proving it. See People (AG) v Cummins [1972] IR 312 and People (DPP) v Shaw [1982] IR 1. Separate from voluntariness, the court retains a discretion to exclude a voluntary statement where, on the whole circumstances, admitting it would be unfair to the accused. • Oppression — conduct that saps the will: prolonged or hostile questioning, deprivation of rest, food or medical attention, intimidation. • Inducement — a threat or promise by a person in authority (“it’ll go easier if you talk”, “you can go home once you sign”). Even a well-intentioned hint can vitiate voluntariness. • Capacity — intoxication, withdrawal, youth, or mental disorder bear on whether the statement was

B.8.2 The voluntariness rule People (DPP) v Shaw [1982] IR 1 — confessions must be voluntary and obtained in compliance with basic fairness; a confession obtained by oppression or by threats or inducements is inadmissible. Oppression, threats, inducements, or conditions that sap the free will of the suspect render a confession involuntary and inadmissible — a question decided on the voir dire (B.1.2). s.t: 2.4 Vehicle Search — Specific Considerations DPP v Owens [1999] 2 IR 16 FACTS: Search of a vehicle at the roadside under s.23 MDA; scope and grounds challenged. PRINCIPLE: Vehicle searches under s.23 require reasonable suspicion that the vehicle contains controlled drugs. The search may include the cab, boot, under-seat areas, glove compartment, and accessible compartments. Locked compartments require specific suspicion to extend the search. RATIO: Vehicle searches are searches — the same standards of grounds, proportionality, and recording apply. TEST: Reasonable suspicion of drugs in the vehicle, with scope proportionate to suspicion. HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence may challenge the basis for stop, the basis for search, the basis for extending search to specific compartments. Each step is articulable. s.t: Operational application: On any shop theft involving entry behind the counter or into a stockroom, consider both s.4 theft and s.12 burglary. Photograph the layout. Get a manager's statement confirming the area was staff-only. The People (AG) v Thompson [1960] IR 70 Facts: Thompson moved goods within a shop with intent to steal them but was apprehended before leaving. The question was whether the offence was complete. Held: The Supreme Court held that asportation — the slightest movement of the property with intent — completes the taking. The offence is complete inside the shop; the defendant need not leave with the property. Ratio: Theft is complete on the slightest movement of property with dishonest intent. Detention need not await the suspect leaving the premises. Operational application: Store security and your statement should record the precise moment of appropriation — where the goods were taken from, where the suspect moved the

The People (AG) v Thompson [1960] IR 70 (SC) S.12 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 (Burglary) Facts, Ratio & Reasoning permanent in all cases — temporary deprivation in certain circumstances may suffice (e.g. taking knowing item will be destroyed). RATIO: Codifies and broadens theft offence. FACTS: Foley charged with theft of vehicle; argued intent to return. PRINCIPLE: The CCA held that intention to permanently deprive is the standard for theft, but the concept is broad. Borrowing with no genuine intent to return = theft. The longer the holding, the deeper the assumption of ownership rights, the stronger the inference of permanent deprivation. RATIO: Permanence is the rule; temporary deprivation may suffice in narrow circumstances. s.t: Part H — Sentencing Principles for Person Crime Investigation feeds into sentencing through the antecedent statement, victim impact statement, and aggravating / mitigating evidence built into the file. The investigating Garda should be familiar with the sentencing framework even though sentencing is judicial. The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250 — Sentencing Proportionality Citation: [1988] IR 250 (Supreme Court). Facts: Sentencing appeal in a serious offence; principles of proportionality were articulated. Holding: Sentences must be proportionate to the offence and the offender. The court must consider both the gravity of the offence and the personal circumstances of the offender. Mitigation is a matter of substance, not merely formality. Significance: Tiernan is the foundational Irish authority on sentencing proportionality. It frames the structureddiscretion approach in Irish sentencing. The People (DPP) v M [1994] 3 IR 306 — Sen

Part F - Sentencing Authority F.1 The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250 Citation: [1988] IR 250 (Supreme Court). Facts: Sentence appeal in a serious sexual offence case. The Supreme Court took the opportunity to set out the structured discretion framework for sentencing. Holding: Sentencing in Ireland is a matter of structured judicial discretion. Identify the gravity of the offence on a scale; identify aggravating factors; identify mitigating factors; arrive at a sentence proportionate to the offence and the offender. Significance: Foundational sentencing framework. Applies across the criminal law including POA offences. Operational Application: File preparation should support the sentencing exercise. Aggravating factors specific to POA offences include: weapons; vulnerable IP; serious injury; pre-planning; persistent / repeat offending; impact on community; racial or other discriminatory motive. Established the Casey warning: where a conviction depends wholly or substantially on visual identification, the jury must be warned of the danger of acting on it, and of the possibility that an honest and convincing witness may nonetheless be mistaken. People (DPP) v Boyce [2008] IESC 62 (adopting R v Turnbull [1977] QB 224) Holding / principle. The court must direct the jury to examine the circumstances of the identification using the Turnbull factors, captured operationally by ADVOKATE: Amount of time under observation, Distance, Visibility, Obstruction, Known to the witness, Any reason to remember, Time lapse since the event, Errors or discrepancies between accounts. Operational point. Take the ADVOKATE detail in the witness statement at the time, in the witness’s own words — not reconstructed later. Part C - Irish Authority on Arrest, Interview, and Evidence C.1 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 Citation: [1980] IR 294 (Supreme Court). Facts: Walsh was arrested on suspicion of a serious crime. The arresting member's grounds were challenged. Issue on appeal: the standard required for the arresting member's reasonable suspicion. Holding: The arresting member must have reasonable suspicion, articulable in fact. The suspicion must be the member's own (not vicarious from a colleague's instruction without further information). The suspicion need not amount to prima facie evidence but must be more than mere conjecture. Reasoning: The Supreme Court framed arrest as a significant intrusion on liberty requiring genuine justification at the time of arrest. The member need not establish the offence at the moment of arrest but must have facts known to them that ground reasonable suspicion. Significance: Foundational Irish authority on the

DPP v Holland (CCA, unreported 15 June 1998) FACTS: Lengthy interview without adequate breaks; suspect exhausted; confession excluded. PRINCIPLE: Excessive interview duration without proper breaks producing exhaustion is oppression. The resulting confession is inadmissible. RATIO: Duration + lack of rest + cumulative pressure = oppression. The Supreme Court considered whether the right to legal advice requires solicitor consultation before interview. PRINCIPLE: There is a constitutional right of reasonable access to legal advice for a person in custody. Where the suspect has requested a solicitor, no questioning may occur until the solicitor has been consulted (or until reasonable efforts to make the solicitor available have been exhausted and the suspect waives the right). RATIO: Solicitor access is a constituent of fair process — investigators must wait, not press ahead. TEST: Did t

The ECtHR considered compatibility with the right to a fair trial under Article 6 ECHR. PRINCIPLE: Inferences from silence are compatible with Article 6 ECHR provided: (a) the suspect had access to a solicitor before being subjected to the inference regime, (b) the court directs itself or the jury appropriately on the dangers of relying solely on silence, (c) other evidence supports the inference, (d) the trial as a whole is fair. s.t: The Architecture of Proof 1.1 Burden and standard of proof The prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt. This is the “golden thread” running through the criminal law — Woolmington v DPP [1935] AC 462 — carried into Irish law and grounded in the right to trial in due course of law under Article 38.1 of the Constitution. “Beyond reasonable doubt” is a high standard: not proof beyond all doubt, but proof to the point where the tribunal is sure. The accused is presumed innocent; if at the end of the case the tribunal is left with a reasonable doubt, the accused is acquitted. Two distinct burdens must be kept apart: • Legal (persuasive) burden — the obligation to prove a fact to the required standard. On the ingredients of the offence this rests on the prosecution from start to finish and never shifts. It is discharged only to the criminal standard. • Evidential burden — the lighter obligation to put enough material before the co

Woolmington v DPP Ratio. The “golden thread”: the prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt; the burden on the elements never shifts to the accused. Apply it. Build every file to stand on prosecution evidence alone. Full treatment: p.845 Working reference - not legal advice - verify before relying. | Page 893

d: Woolmington v DPP [1935] AC 462 — the ‘golden thread’ — the prosecution must prove guilt; the accused need not prove innocence.

s.t: The ordinary direction — that the prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt — suffices; the judge is not obliged, as a separate rule of law, to direct that the facts must be inconsistent with any rational conclusion other than guilt. Operational point. The protection for the accused is the criminal standard itself, applied to the whole circumstantial picture — not a magic formula. The People (DPP) v Wyse [2020] IECA 190 Holding. Birmingham P confirmed there is no general practice of giving a special warning about circumstantial evidence; how the evidence is addressed is a matter for the trial judge in the particular case.

d: The People (DPP) v Wyse [2020] IECA 190 Holding.

DPP v Yates [2010] IECCA FACTS: Prosecution witness gave evidence inconsistent with prior statement; application to treat as hostile and admit prior statement. PRINCIPLE: Where witness inconsistent at trial, court may rule hostile, permitting crossexamination by calling party. Prior statement may be admitted under s.16 CJA 2006. RATIO: Witness retraction managed via hostile witness procedure and s.16. PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Prepare for the possibility — every DV statement, every gangrelated witness statement, every vulnerable witness. Best evidence at the time. CROSS-REFERENCES: s.16 CJA 2006; DV practice. 4.6 Disclosure — The

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Yates (hostile witness); s.16 CJA 2006 (sworn statements); DPP v Tuite. Section 16, Criminal Justice Act 2006 — Sworn Statement Discipline Statutory framework FACTS: (See also Evidence section.) Sworn statement procedure designed to support admissibility where witness later retracts or becomes hostile. PRINCIPLE: A sworn statement made before a peace commissioner, in circumstances of voluntariness and reliability, may be admitted as substantive evidence where the witness at trial refuses to give evidence, gives materially inconsistent evidence, or denies making the prior statement. PRACTICAL APPLICATION: DV cases especially: take sworn statement (s.16 format) at first opportunity. Peace commissioner administers oath. Statement covers al

Notebook entry on circumstances. COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Cursory s.16 statement that wouldn't stand alone. (2) Statement taken in unsuitable conditions. (3) Failure to take s.16 statement when circumstances clearly warrant. CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Yates; hostile witness procedure; DV practice. 9.2 Vulnerable Witnesses Criminal Evidence Act 1992 — Special Measures Statutory framework FACTS: Provides special measures for vulnerable witnesses: video link, screens, intermediary, removal of formal court dress, written statements as evidence in chief. PRINCIPLE: Where giving evidence in open court would cause significant distress or impair quality of evidence, special measures available to enable best evidence. Application to court for measures; threshold varies. PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Identify vulnerability early — at statement-taking stage. Document on file. Special measures application via prosecutor in advance of trial. Coordinate with court
